title: Assign statement line coverage missing
body:
Environment: 
* verilator version - `Verilator 4.222 2022-05-02 rev v4.222-24-g4618fd5a`
* OS - Windows 11, wsl2 Ubuntu-18.04
* 
I have a simple circuit:

```verilog
module cell_inv (
    input  wire a,         //% a input.
    output wire z          //% z output, z = ~a;
    );
    assign z = ~a;
endmodule //cell_inv
```
I have a testbench that tests the karnaugh map and verilate it with `--coverage` to ensure both line and toggle coverage.

My C++ testbench instantiates the necessary verilator code and verilated cell_inv
```cpp
    std::unique_ptr<VerilatedContext> m_context; ///< Verilator Context
    std::unique_ptr<VerilatedVcdC>    m_trace;   ///< Trace Class variable
    std::unique_ptr<T>                m_dut;     ///< Device Under Test (DUT)
 ```
And these are setup appropriately for execution for vcd generation and writing out the coverage at the end of the test.

```cpp
m_dut->a = 0
m_context->timeInc(1);
m_dut->eval();
m_dut->a = 1
m_context->timeInc(1);
m_dut->eval()

//on completion:
m_context->coveragep()->write(m_covName.c_str());
```
I then use this to generate lcov/genhtml coverage and notice a few things:

1. there is no line coverage for the assign statement - is this expected?
The `verilog_coverage --annotate-all --annotate annotate test.data` command is called and the output is:

```
        module cell_inv (
%000002     input  wire a,         //% a input.
%000003     output wire z          //% z output, z = ~a;
            );
            assign z = ~a;
        endmodule //cell_buf
```

I see the coverage data file has only toggle instances, and there is no line coverage for the assignment.

Thanks.

A few bonus questions/suggestions:

1. When generating the genhtml - the line and toggle coverage is amalgamated into one group called `Hit`  I was expecting them to be separate groups - Line and toggle - which would be helpful.  eg: Line, Toggle, branch(later).
2. Also toggle coverage typically separates out `0->1` and `1->0` counts - but it's not a big deal having the aggregate like it is right now.
